feedback.text: In principle, I find the Digital Fairness Act a very good and important initiative. At a time when more and more digital products and services are being used, it is important to protect consumers’ rights here too. The approach of strengthening fairness, transparency and responsibility in the digital space is absolutely right and forward-looking. However, I would like the Digital Fairness Act to take greater account of the handling of purchased digital games and software, in particular when switching off or deleting products that consumers have already purchased. A specific example: I bought the game The Crew offered by Ubisoft some time ago. Although I paid for the game, it has now been completely removed and is no longer playable even in a single player mode. This means that, as a buyer, I no longer have the possibility to use the product even though I bought it legally. I consider this to be unfair and disappointing. I would therefore very much welcome that the Digital Fairness Act also contains provisions to ensure that digital products that have been purchased cannot simply be rendered inoperable or deleted after the event, at least not without adequate compensation or compensation. Buyers should be allowed to enjoy a certain level of digital property security. A possible solution for online games would be if the Digital Fairness Act sets clear guidelines for fair treatment of the so-called end-of-life plan. More specifically, provision could be made for: An obligation to provide dedicated server software or offline mode as soon as a game is permanently stopped. An early and transparent information on planned server shutdowns ideally before purchase. Such measures would significantly strengthen digital consumer sovereignty and ensure that buyers are not left behind with an unusable product years later. Thank you for your work on this important issue! Yours sincerely, Christian Uliczka
